title: Kundalini and Creativity: The Art That Comes After the Crisis

body:
Kundalini and Creativity: The Art That Comes After the Crisis

You're probably reading this because you've been there, or you're there right now. That buzzing, that heat, the terror that feels like it's ripping you apart from the inside. They call it Kundalini, spiritual awakening, enlightenment. You know what I call it? A goddamn nightmare when it goes sideways. I've been there. I've felt that electricity trying to blow circuits in my brain, the panic that makes you think you're going insane, the isolation that screams you're utterly alone in this. And you are, mostly. But here's the thing: you're not the only one. And after the dust settles, after you claw your way back from the brink, something incredible can happen. You might find art. You might find expression you never knew you had.

Look, when your system gets overloaded, when that energy blasts through you, it doesn't just mess with your nervous system. It cracks open everything. Your perceptions, your beliefs, your very sense of self. It's like a cosmic pressure washer cleaning out all the gunk, but it's not gentle. It's violent. It's disorienting. And once it's done, or at least, once the initial storm passes, you're left with a world that's been scorched and then strangely, beautifully, fertile. That's where creativity comes in. That's where you start to rebuild, not just yourself, but your world, through whatever means you can grab onto.

I'm not talking about some airy-fairy, "everything happens for a reason" bullshit. I'm talking about the raw, visceral need to make sense of what you've been through. The urge to translate the unspeakable into something tangible. Because if you don't, it just sits there, festering, making you feel crazy all over again. You've got to get it out. You just have to.

The Aftermath: When Your Old Life Doesn't Fit

You know that feeling, right? You've gone through this experience, this internal earthquake, and you look around at your old life, your old friends, your old job, and it all just feels... wrong. Like wearing clothes that are three sizes too small. You've seen too much, felt too much. The mundane feels hollow, the trivial feels insulting. You're different. You can't go back. And that can be just as terrifying as the crisis itself. Because what now? Who are you now?

This is where many people get stuck. They try to force themselves back into the old molds, and it just doesn't work. It creates more pain, more frustration. The energy that's been awakened, even if it's settled down, still wants to flow. It wants to express. It wants to create. If you try to suppress it, it'll find other ways to manifest - sometimes as anxiety, sometimes as depression, sometimes as physical ailments. It's like trying to cap a volcano. You can't. It's just going to build pressure until it explodes again. You've got to give it an outlet. You need to channel it.

And honestly, you might not even feel like you want to create at first. You might just feel numb, or exhausted, or just plain confused. That's okay. The urge will come. It might come as a whisper, a tiny itch. Don't ignore it. That little itch is the seed of something new, something vital. It's your soul trying to communicate, trying to integrate what's happened. It's trying to heal.

Finding Your Voice Again

After a kundalini crisis, your voice might feel lost. Or it might feel like it's screaming, but no one's listening. Or maybe you're just too tired to speak. I get it. I really do. For me, it was like someone had scrambled all the words in my head. I couldn't string a sentence together. It was terrifying. I used to be a writer, a talker, and suddenly, silence. But the silence wasn't empty. It was full of raw, unformed energy. It was like a wellspring that had just been uncapped, and I just didn't know how to draw from it yet.

So, what do you do? You start small. You don't try to write the great American novel or paint a masterpiece. You just make a mark. You scribble. You hum. You move your body. Anything. It doesn't have to be good. It doesn't have to make sense to anyone else. It just has to be yours. It has to be an outward manifestation of that inner turmoil, that inner growth. It's about giving form to the formless. It's about taking that chaotic energy and giving it a container.

I found myself drawing. I couldn't draw for shit before. But suddenly, my hands just wanted to move. I'd sit there with a pen and paper, and lines would just appear. Mandalas, abstract shapes, things that felt like echoes of the internal patterns I was seeing. It wasn't about being an artist. It was about release. It was about giving that energy a pathway out of my body and onto the page. You might find yourself drawn to something similar, like a Mandala Coloring Book for Adults - Stress Relief (paid link). It's simple, it's calming, and it gives your hands something to do while your mind processes.

The Creative Urge as Integration

Here's the thing: when you go through a spiritual emergency, your consciousness expands. You see things differently. You feel things differently. The boundaries between you and everything else can blur. It's overwhelming. It's beautiful. It's terrifying. And your old ways of understanding the world just don't cut it anymore. Your brain is trying to make sense of something it wasn't designed to comprehend. So, what do you do? You create new ways of understanding. You create new maps. That's what art is. It's a way of mapping the unseen, of giving form to the ineffable.

Think of it like this: your experience is a massive, complex jigsaw puzzle, but all the pieces are blank. And you're sitting there, trying to put it together, but you don't even know what the picture is supposed to be. Creativity is the act of drawing on those blank pieces. It's the act of giving them color, meaning, connection. It's how you integrate. It's how you bring those fragmented experiences back into a cohesive whole. It's how you heal.

It's not about being "good" at art. It's about the process. It's about the act of creation itself. It's about the feeling of moving energy through your hands, through your voice, through your body. It's about connecting with that deeper part of you that knows, even when your conscious mind is still reeling. It's about finding solace in the act of making. You're literally creating yourself anew.

Journaling: Your First Line of Defense

I can't stress this enough: write it down. Even if it's just nonsense. Even if it's just a stream of consciousness that makes no sense to anyone else. Especially if it makes no sense. Get it out of your head and onto the page. Your thoughts, your fears, your weird visions, the physical sensations - everything. Don't censor yourself. Don't edit. Just write. This is what Julia Cameron calls "Morning Pages," and it's a lifesaver. You can even get a dedicated The Artist's Way Morning Pages Journal by Julia Cameron (paid link) to help you start the habit.

I remember sitting there, shaking, tears streaming down my face, just scribbling. Sometimes it was just loops and squiggles. Sometimes it was angry words. Sometimes it was just "I'm scared. I'm so scared." Over and over. But every time I did it, I felt a tiny bit lighter. A tiny bit clearer. It's like you're draining the swamp of your mind. You're giving those frantic thoughts a place to land, so they're not just bouncing around in your skull, driving you crazy. It's a way to witness your own experience without judgment. It's a way to process the unprocessed.

And you know what? Sometimes, buried in that mess, you'll find a gem. A phrase, an image, an idea that sparks something else. That's the beginning. That's the new growth pushing through the scorched earth. That's the creativity emerging from the crisis.

The Many Faces of Post-Crisis Art

So, what kind of art are we talking about? Anything. Absolutely anything that helps you express, process, and integrate. There's no right or wrong way to do this. Your experience is unique, and your expression will be too. It might be obvious, like painting or music. Or it might be something more subtle, more personal.

  • Writing: Beyond journaling, maybe it's poetry. Maybe it's short stories. Maybe it's just writing letters to yourself that you never send. It's about finding the words to describe the indescribable.
  • Visual Arts: Drawing, painting, sculpting, photography. Giving form to the visions, the feelings, the energy. It doesn't have to be realistic. Abstract can be incredibly powerful for this kind of experience.
  • Music/Sound: Singing, playing an instrument, even just humming or making sounds. The vibrations can be incredibly healing and help to move stagnant energy. Many find themselves drawn to sound healing after such an experience.
  • Movement/Dance: Your body holds so much of the trauma, the energy. Moving it, letting it express freely, can be deeply liberating. Don't worry about looking good. Just move. Let your body tell its story.
  • Crafts/Building: Knitting, pottery, woodworking, gardening. The act of creating something tangible, something with your hands, can be incredibly grounding. It brings you back into the physical world when you feel like you're floating away.
  • Cooking/Baking: Yes, even this. It's a creative act. It engages your senses, it requires focus, and it produces something nourishing. It's a simple, comforting way to bring creativity into your daily life.

The key is to find what calls to you. What feels like a release? What feels like a way to channel that intense energy? What makes you feel a tiny bit more whole, a tiny bit more sane? Start there. Don't overthink it. Just do it. You're not trying to be famous. You're trying to survive, and then you're trying to thrive. And creativity is a huge part of that.

I'll tell you what, after going through what I did, I never thought I'd find joy again. I thought I'd be stuck in that terrifying place forever. But honestly, the art, the writing, the expression - it was like a lifeline. It was the way back. It was the way forward. It was the way to integrate the pieces of myself that had been blown apart. And it will be for you too, if you let it. You just have to be brave enough to pick up the pen, or the brush, or just hum a tune, and let that energy flow. It's a part of your healing journey, a part of making sense of the unusual, terrifying, and ultimately, deeply spiritual experience you've had.

Honoring the Process, Not the Product

One of the biggest traps you can fall into is judging your creative output. "This isn't good enough." "I'm not an artist." "What's the point?" Stop. Just stop that right now. This isn't about creating something for public consumption. This is about your sanity. This is about your healing. The product is secondary, if it matters at all. The process is everything.

When you're in the midst of a spiritual crisis, or reeling from one, you're fragile. You're raw. Your inner critic is probably already having a field day with all the other stuff you're going through. Don't give it more ammunition. Be kind to yourself. Treat this creative exploration like a sacred space, a sanctuary where judgment isn't allowed. This is for you, and only for you.

It's about showing up. It's about making time, even when you're exhausted, even when you're scared. Even if it's just five minutes of scribbling. That consistent act of creation, that consistent channeling of energy, that's what builds momentum. That's what helps you integrate. That's what helps you find your footing again in this new, strange world you inhabit.

And know what? Sometimes, in the act of creating, you'll stumble upon insights. You'll see connections you didn't see before. You'll understand something about your experience that words alone couldn't convey. The art speaks to you, just as you're speaking through it. It's a dialogue with your deeper self, with the universe, with whatever force brought you to this place. It's a way of making peace with the storm you've weathered.

If you're feeling utterly lost, and you're struggling to even begin, sometimes reading about others' experiences can help. Books like Spiritual Emergency: When Personal Transformation Becomes a Crisis (paid link) or Waking Up Is Hard to Do: Navigating Spiritual Emergence (paid link) can offer some comfort in knowing you're not alone and give you ideas for how others have coped and found their way back. They talk about the importance of expression, of finding ways to process the overwhelming. It's not just about surviving the crisis, it's about making something out of it. It's about turning the pain into purpose, the chaos into creation.

So yeah, you've been through hell. You might still be in it. But there's a way out, and there's a way through. And often, that way is paved with whatever you can create. Let that energy flow. Let it transform you, not just internally, but externally. Let it become your art. Let it be your healing. You've got this. You really do.

This is not a substitute for professional mental health care. If you're in crisis, please reach out to a qualified therapist or call 988.
author: Kalesh
